nativeRange.habitat: Shallow margins of ponds, slow streams, and seasonally flooded wetlands; the genus Eleocharis ("marsh dweller") is broadly aquatic-to-mesic and pusilla is described by Wikipedia/AusFlora as a temperate Australasian spikerush of damp soils and shallow standing water

decorPreferences: fine sand or small-grain substrate to anchor delicate roots
open foreground with strong direct light
stable, clean water column with gentle flow

avoidWith: boisterous fish that uproot fine carpet plants

imageCandidates.notes: Retailer product page photo of tissue-culture Eleocharis pusilla 'Mini' carpet. Watermarked retailer imagery, used as a visual candidate only.

summary: Eleocharis pusilla 'Mini' is the true dwarf form of the hairgrass family — a finer, shorter cousin of Dwarf Hairgrass (E. parvula) used as a foreground carpet. It stays around 3–5 cm tall and forms a dense, lawn-like mat under high light and pressurised CO2, but is rated demanding outside high-tech setups.
careNotes: Eleocharis pusilla 'Mini' (sedge family Cyperaceae, order Poales) is a small Australasian spikerush marketed in the aquarium hobby as the 'true dwarf' hairgrass — distinctly shorter and finer than the more common Eleocharis parvula (Dwarf Hairgrass) and the larger Eleocharis acicularis (Needle Spikerush). Mature blades typically stay around 3–5 cm (1–2 in), making this one of the smallest hairgrasses available and ideal for foreground carpeting in iwagumi and nature-style aquascapes where scale matters. Aquarium Co-Op describes it as medium-to-high light, CO2-recommended, slow-growing, and propagating readily by short runners from a tissue-culture portion that can be separated into many small plugs spaced 2–4 cm apart. In line with the broader Eleocharis carpet plants, care is generally considered demanding: pressurised CO2 (~15–25 mg/L) plus strong direct light over the foreground are widely recommended to keep the carpet compact and prevent it from melting back or being overrun by algae during establishment. A fine, nutrient-rich substrate (aquasoil) supports the delicate root system; root tabs and a balanced column dosing of macros and trace iron help. Temperature 20–27 °C, pH ~6.0–7.5, and soft-to-moderately-hard water (gH ~2–10) suit it; it tolerates a fairly wide hardness range but does best in soft, slightly acidic water. The dry-start method — growing emersed on damp aquasoil under a sealed lid for 4–8 weeks — is commonly used to establish a dense, well-rooted carpet before flooding. Once carpeted, trimming is minimal thanks to its small mature height; occasional light pruning keeps the mat tidy and lets light reach the lower blades. Demanding: best avoided as a first carpet plant unless CO2, light, and nutrients can be held stable.
breedingNotes: Propagation is vegetative and spreads horizontally via short runners (stolons), which is how a planted carpet thickens and expands across the foreground. Aquascapers typically start from tissue-culture cups, split the portion into many small clumps of a few stems each, and plant them 2–4 cm apart so runners knit the foreground together over weeks to months. Dry start (emersed growth on damp substrate under a sealed lid) is widely used to establish a dense, well-rooted carpet before flooding the tank. No sexual propagation is used in the hobby.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op
primary.notes: Retailer product page for tissue-culture Eleocharis pusilla 'Mini'. Source for adult height (~1–2 in / 3–5 cm), medium-to-high lighting requirement, recommended CO2 supplementation, slow growth rate, foreground/carpet placement, nutrient-rich substrate, and propagation by separating tissue-culture plugs that spread by runners.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Eleocharis
additional.notes: Used for genus-level taxonomy (family Cyperaceae, order Poales — the sedge family within the grass-like Poales), the broadly aquatic-to-mesic 'marsh dweller' habitat profile (sea level to 5,000+ m), the confirmation that Eleocharis pusilla R.Br. is a recognised species in the genus, and disambiguation against the better-known hobby species E. acicularis (Needle Spikerush) and E. parvula (Dwarf Spikerush / Dwarf Hairgrass) for which pusilla is the smaller cousin.
